React, Why Not Learn It Now? - The React Router Hooks You'll Actually Use - (27)
React, Why Not Learn It Now?

Since we've been doing everything the Hook way in this series, it makes sense that React Router ships its own set of Hooks too. In this post I'll walk through the ones you'll bump into most often — useLocation, useParams, useNavigate, and useRoutes — with small examples for each so you know what they do and when to reach for them.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Router - Link and NavLink - (26)
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now?

We touched on Link earlier, but we never really got to know it properly — we just showed how to use it and moved on. So this post takes a closer look at Link, and along the way introduces another piece: NavLink. We'll walk through Link's props (including the tricky state one in React Router v6), then build a navbar with NavLink so it can track the current page and style itself accordingly.
React, Why Not Learn It Now? - React Router - Nested Routes - (25)

We've already covered the basics of React Router, so now it's time for another concept: nested routes. Nested routes show up all the time in real-world development, so this post walks through what they are, how to set them up, and why the `<Outlet />` component is the missing piece that makes shared layouts work.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Router: The Basics - (24)

For tiny projects, everything we've covered so far is honestly more than enough. But real projects don't work like that — as the app grows, your pages get more and more complex, and at some point you need a tool to help you manage them. That tool is React Router. In this post I'll introduce what it is, walk through SPA basics, and get a minimal router up and running.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - Vite: Why It's So Fast & Deploying to GitHub Pages - (23)

In the last chapter we got our Vite + React + Tailwind CSS environment ready, so now it's time to move everything over from CRA to Vite. After that, we'll take a quick look at why Vite is so fast under the hood, and walk through two different ways to deploy the project to GitHub Pages.
React, You'll Learn It Eventually — Why Not Now? - Setting Up React with Vite - (22)
React, You'll Learn It Eventually — Why Not Now?

This is where we kick off a whole new chapter: Vite. A lot of you have probably already used Vite on a project, and if you haven't, no worries — I'll walk you through why Vite is so nice to work with, and how to actually use it. We'll build a Vite-for-React project from scratch, poke at its folder structure, and wire up Tailwind CSS along the way.
You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - Moving the TodoList into CRA and Deploying to GitHub Pages - (21)

In this chapter we're taking the TodoList we built back on day eleven and moving it over from CodePen into a real Create React App (CRA) project. Along the way we'll tidy up the CRA boilerplate, add Tailwind CSS so the TodoList actually looks decent, and then walk through deploying the whole thing to GitHub Pages step by step. By the end you'll have a real, shareable URL for your TodoList.
React, Might As Well Learn It Now - StrictMode and Web Vitals in CRA (20)
React, Might As Well Learn It Now

Picking up from the last post's CRA project, today we'll finally go through the remaining 20% of the generated code we haven't looked at yet. So this one is going to be pretty light and easy. We'll cover what reportWebVitals (Web Vitals) actually does, what React.StrictMode is for, and why your useEffect sometimes runs twice.
Teachable Video Shows Blank? Browser Strict Privacy Settings Are the Likely Cause

Some readers told me Teachable videos suddenly stopped playing, but my own browser was fine. Turned out the culprit was their browser's Strict privacy mode. This post walks through the fix in Firefox, Chrome, and Edge, plus the fallback when toggling privacy mode isn't enough.
Learn React #19 — Spinning Up a Project with Create React App (CRA)

This kicks off a brand new chapter — the Create React App (CRA) chapter. From here on we'll slowly move away from CodePen and start working in the kind of development environment you'd actually use for real projects. I'll walk through what CRA is, how to spin up a project, and what the generated folder structure looks like.
